nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes, lagoons, and bays at depths typically 2–15 m (recorded 0–30 m); tends to hover 1–2 m above sand or rubble bottoms feeding on suspended zooplankton

decorPreferences: open sand bed at least 2 inches deep for burrowing
live rock with crevices and overhangs for shelter
ample open swimming space above the substrate
tight-fitting lid required to prevent jumping

diet.notes: Has been documented in the wild hovering 1–2 m above the substrate feeding on zooplankton. In captivity generally accepts mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafoods, and frozen carnivore preparations. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised to mimic continuous planktivory.

summary: The Scissortail Dartfish is a peaceful, reef-safe Indo-Pacific dartfish with a striking white-and-black body and forked tail. Generally considered beginner-friendly, but it is a notorious jumper, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised.
careNotes:
Generally advised to keep in an established reef or fish-only aquarium of at least roughly 120 L (about 30 US gal), with a deep open sand bed for burrowing and live rock providing crevices for shelter. This species tends to dart into rockwork or a burrow when startled and may be shy for the first several days after introduction.

Widely regarded as a jumper — a tight-fitting lid with no gaps is generally considered essential. Escape risk is high, consistent with most dartfish in the hobby.

Best kept singly or as an established mated pair; have been known to be aggressive toward conspecifics that are not bonded mates, so adding multiple individuals later can lead to fighting. Generally tolerant of other dartfish species and broadly peaceful toward unrelated tankmates.

Source cross-check: LiveAquaria cites 5.5 in (≈14 cm) maximum and 30 US gal minimum at 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase reports a maximum of 14 cm TL and a wild temperature envelope of 25.8–29.3°C. The temperature range used here (23–27°C) averages hobby and wild figures. Tank minimum is biased slightly upward from the strict LiveAquaria 113.6 L to 120 L to reflect this species' need for open swimming space above the substrate.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been well documented in commonly available hobbyist sources. In the wild, adults have been reported to occur in monogamous pairs sharing a burrow, and juveniles tend to form small aggregations. No established aquarium breeding protocol exists in the typical hobby literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet on the per-species product page (/products/scissortail-dartfish) supplied sizing, tank minimum, water parameters, temperament, diet, reef-safe status, and care level.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy (Gobiiformes / Microdesmidae), max length 14 cm TL, native range (Red Sea and East Africa to Line and Society Islands, north to Ryukyu and Ogasawara, south to NSW and Rapa), reef-slope/lagoon habitat, wild temperature 25.8–29.3°C, planktivorous diet, and monogamous pairing behavior all cross-checked.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning of this species is not documented in typical hobby literature. Wild adults form monogamous pairs that share a burrow, but no aquarium breeding protocol is established.
